summary.brief: Autieri v. Rosen. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: The defendant Mitchell K. Rosen, as the plaintiff's primary care physician, ordered blood-work panels for the plaintiff in February 2016, March 2017, and August 2018 to test for prostate-specific androgen (hereinafter PSA), among other things.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In April 2017, Rosen referred the plaintiff to a urologist but made no mention of the plaintiff's elevated PSA level, only listing hematuria as the reason for the referral.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In November 2019, following a prostate biopsy and more blood work, the plaintiff was diagnosed with prostate cancer.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The defendants' expert affidavit addressed the specific allegations in the plaintiff's bill of particulars and explained that the standard of care for a primary care physician in the scenario herein was to make a referral to a specialist and to allow the specialist to conduct further testing.
evidence_index.speaker: Defendants' expert

evidence_index.text: The plaintiff's expert explained that, at the plaintiff's initial consultation with the urologist, the plaintiff's elevated PSA level was not addressed because Rosen failed to inform the first urologist of the plaintiff's elevated PSA level.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we reverse the order and deny the defendants' motion for summary judgment dismissing the complaint insofar as asserted against them.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient had elevated PSA levels in March 2017 and August 2018, but was referred to a urologist for hematuria without mention of elevated PSA.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

critical_findings: Elevated PSA levels not communicated to urologist

reveal.result_summary: Elevated PSA levels in March 2017 and August 2018
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation or referral with full information

reveal.result_summary: Initial consultation did not address elevated PSA
reveal.clinical_significance: Missed opportunity to investigate potential prostate cancer
reveal.decision_impact: Could have led to earlier diagnosis if PSA was communicated

options.description: Include elevated PSA levels in referral

options.clinical_reasoning: Incomplete information may lead to missed diagnosis

expected_action_court.description: Referral should have included elevated PSA levels

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to communicate elevated PSA levels led to delayed diagnosis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral should have included all relevant lab results

patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Potential progression of cancer due to delayed diagnosis

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to communicate elevated PSA levels
Causation established - earlier communication could have led to earlier diagnosis

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier communication could have prompted timely diagnosis
